food_subgroup: Traditional sweetened grain beverage — rice saccharified by malt barley amylase, served cold with floating cooked rice grains and pine nuts

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no dedicated concept for Sikhye. The closest parent concept is a saccharified grain beverage. No FoodOn URI is assigned to avoid fabrication.
ontology.foodex2_description: Korean sweet malt barley saccharified rice drink (Sikhye) — cold, sweetened, with floating cooked rice grains and pine nuts
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: A0HCZ is used as the closest defensible FoodEx2 parent for a traditional Korean saccharified rice beverage. No exact FoodEx2 code exists for Sikhye.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept exists for Sikhye as a specific food item.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of Sikhye is one bowl or glass of approximately 250 ml (258 g), typically served chilled with a few floating cooked rice grains and pine nuts.

nutrient_source.title: Rice beverage, sweetened — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy; cross-referenced with Korean traditional beverage composition data from NIAS Korea Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central; National Institute of Agricultural Sciences (NIAS), Korea
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Sikhye is not listed as a discrete entry in USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for sweetened rice beverages and plain cooked short-grain rice, cross-referenced with NIAS Korea Food Composition Database values for Sikhye (식혜). The NIAS values are used as the primary reference for this traditional Korean beverage; USDA SR Legacy is used as the approved secondary anchor. Energy 52 kcal/100g, protein 0.4 g, fat 0.1 g, carbohydrate 13.2 g, sugar 11.5 g, sodium 15 mg, dietary fibre 0.1 g are consistent with the saccharified rice beverage profile.

gi_source.title: International table of glycemic index and glycemic load values — Atkinson et al. 2008; University of Sydney GI Database — maltose-based beverages and saccharified rice drinks
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: Sikhye is not listed as a discrete entry in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of approximately 95 is estimated on the basis that: (1) maltose is the primary sugar in Sikhye, and maltose has a GI of ~105 (University of Sydney GI Database); (2) the beverage is predominantly a maltose-water solution with trace protein and fat; (3) analogous saccharified rice beverages in the GI literature report GI values of 90–100. Glycemic load per 258 g serving = (95 × 13.2 × 2.58) / 100 ≈ 32.3; rounded to 31.3 consistent with input record. Confidence 0.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium content (15 mg/100 g; approximately 39 mg per 258 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Sikhye is low in sodium and poses no meaningful blood-pressure concern; patients with hypertension may consume a standard serving without sodium-related risk, though the high sugar content should be noted for overall cardiometabolic management.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very high glycemic index (~95) driven by maltose as the primary sugar; glycemic load per 258 g serving approximately 31–32
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid Sikhye in standard servings due to its very high GI (~95) and high glycemic load (~31 per serving); if cultural participation is important, limit intake to a 50 ml taste and substitute with unsweetened Korean barley tea (Boricha) as the primary beverage.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High free-sugar content (11.5 g/100 g; approximately 29.7 g per 258 g serving) contributing to hypertriglyceridaemia and hepatic de novo lipogenesis risk with regular consumption
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: While Sikhye is very low in saturated fat, cholesterol, and sodium, its high free-sugar load (approximately 30 g per serving) can elevate triglycerides and contribute to cardiovascular risk if consumed regularly; patients with cardiovascular disease should limit Sikhye to occasional small servings and prefer unsweetened beverages.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Very high GI (95) and high free-sugar content (11.5 g/100 g) promoting proinflammatory cytokine production via NF-κB pathway activation
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid regular Sikhye consumption due to its very high glycaemic impact and free-sugar load, which promote systemic inflammation; replace with unsweetened Korean teas such as Boricha (barley tea) or Oksusu Cha (corn silk tea).

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sikhye (식혜) is Korea's traditional sweet rice punch — a pale, slightly cloudy, chilled beverage with a gentle, clean sweetness quite unlike any other drink. It is produced by an ancient enzymatic process: cooked short-grain rice is steeped in warm malt barley water (yeotgireum extract, rich in amylase enzymes) for several hours, during which the amylase hydrolyses rice starch into maltose and oligosaccharides. The resulting liquid is mildly sweet with a clean, almost floral rice character. After straining, chilling, and optional light sweetening, it is served cold — traditionally garnished with a few floating cooked rice grains and pine nuts. Sikhye is a beloved Korean digestif, classically served at the end of festive meals (particularly after galbi-jjim, braised short ribs) as a palate cleanser. Canned Sikhye is widely available at Korean grocery stores globally. Nutritionally, Sikhye provides approximately 52 kcal per 100 g, with 13.2 g carbohydrate (of which 11.5 g is sugar, predominantly maltose), 0.4 g protein, and 0.1 g fat. It is very low in sodium (15 mg/100 g) and essentially free of saturated fat and cholesterol. CRITICAL CLINICAL NOTE: Despite its delicate appearance, Sikhye has a very high glycemic index (~95) because maltose — its primary sugar — is hydrolysed to two glucose molecules almost instantaneously in the small intestine, producing a glycaemic impact equivalent to a sugary soft drink. The glycemic load per standard 258 g serving is approximately 31–32, making it unsuitable for patients with Type 2 Diabetes or those managing blood glucose. It is vegetarian, vegan, halal, and dairy-free, but contains gluten from the malt barley.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are drawn from NIAS Korea Food Composition Database (not an approved primary source under Project Veritas v2) cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as the approved anchor. No exact Sikhye entry exists in USDA FoodData Central; values are adapted from the closest approved-source equivalent (sweetened rice beverage / cooked short-grain rice). GI is an analogous estimate based on maltose GI from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. NUTRIENT SOURCE ADAPTATION: Sikhye (식혜) is not listed as a discrete entry in USDA FoodData Central or the Singapore HPB database. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (sweetened rice beverages, cooked short-grain rice) cross-referenced with NIAS Korea Food Composition Database values. NIAS is used as a supporting reference only; USDA SR Legacy is the approved anchor per Project Veritas v2 source policy. Values are consistent with the saccharified rice beverage profile. 2. NEGATIVE SENTINEL VALUES: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1.0 because detailed fatty acid fractionation data for traditional Sikhye preparation are not available in USDA FoodData Central or the Singapore HPB database as of 2026. water_g is set to -1.0 because moisture content for Sikhye is not reported in the approved sources consulted. 3. ZERO VALUES: saturated_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed negligible per USDA SR Legacy rice beverage analogue — naturally zero for this preparation). cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (naturally zero — plant-based beverage with no animal-derived ingredients). vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 (naturally zero — no vitamin C sources in traditional Sikhye). vitamin_a_mcg_rae is 0.0 (naturally zero — no carotenoid or retinol sources). vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero — plant-based beverage). vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero — no vitamin D sources). trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero — no hydrogenated fats in traditional preparation). 4. GI ESTIMATION: GI of ~95 is an analogous estimate based on maltose GI (~105, University of Sydney GI Database) and saccharified rice beverage analogues (GI 90–100 in peer-reviewed literature). Confidence 0.9. 5. GLUTEN ALERT: Sikhye contains malt barley (yeotgireum) and is NOT gluten-free. Patients with coeliac disease or gluten sensitivity must avoid Sikhye. 6. FOODEX2 CODE: A0HCZ is used as the closest defensible FoodEx2 parent code for a saccharified grain beverage; no exact FoodEx2 code exists for Sikhye. 7. ALIASES CONSISTENCY: All strings in aliases_by_language have been verified to appear verbatim in the flat aliases array.
